title: VI: The Vicar and the Angel
html:
VI

The Vicar and the Angel

Now there are some things frankly impossible. The weakest intellect will admit this situation is impossible. The Athenaeum will probably say as much should it venture to review this. Sunbespattered ferns, spreading beech trees, the Vicar and the gun are acceptable enough. But this Angel is a different matter. Plain sensible people will scarcely go on with such an extravagant book. And the Vicar fully appreciated this impossibility. But he lacked decision. Consequently he went on with it, as you shall immediately hear. He was hot, it was after dinner, he was in no mood for mental subtleties. The Angel had him at a disadvantage, and further distracted him from the main issue by irrelevant iridescence and a violent fluttering. For the moment it never occurred to the Vicar to ask whether the Angel was possible or not. He accepted him in the confusion of the moment, and the mischief was done. Put yourself in his place, my dear Athenaeum. You go out shooting. You hit something. That alone would disconcert you. You find you have hit an Angel, and he writhes about for a minute and then sits up and addresses you. He makes no apology for his own impossibility. Indeed, he carries the charge clean into your camp. ‚ÄúA man!‚Äù he says, pointing. ‚ÄúA man in the maddest black clothes and without a feather upon him. Then I was not deceived. I am indeed in the Land of Dreams!‚Äù You must answer him. Unless you take to your heels. Or blow his brains out with your second barrel as an escape from the controversy.

‚ÄúThe Land of Dreams! Pardon me if I suggest you have just come out of it,‚Äù was the Vicar‚Äôs remark.

‚ÄúHow can that be?‚Äù said the Angel.

‚ÄúYour wing,‚Äù said the Vicar, ‚Äúis bleeding. Before we talk, may I have the pleasureÔªø‚Äîthe melancholy pleasureÔªø‚Äîof tying it up? I am really most sincerely sorry.Ôªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶‚Äù The Angel put his hand behind his back and winced.

The Vicar assisted his victim to stand up. The Angel turned gravely and the Vicar, with numberless insignificant panting parentheses, carefully examined the injured wings. (They articulated, he observed with interest, to a kind of second glenoid on the outer and upper edge of the shoulder blade. The left wing had suffered little except the loss of some of the primary wing-quills, and a shot or so in the ala spuria, but the humerus bone of the right was evidently smashed.) The Vicar stanched the bleeding as well as he could and tied up the bone with his pocket handkerchief and the neck wrap his housekeeper made him carry in all weathers.

‚ÄúI‚Äôm afraid you will not be able to fly for some time,‚Äù said he, feeling the bone.

‚ÄúI don‚Äôt like this new sensation,‚Äù said the Angel.

‚ÄúThe pain when I feel your bone?‚Äù

‚ÄúThe what?‚Äù said the Angel.

‚ÄúThe pain.‚Äù

‚Äú‚Ää‚ÄòPain‚ÄôÔªø‚Äîyou call it. No, I certainly don‚Äôt like the Pain. Do you have much of this Pain in the Land of Dreams?‚Äù

‚ÄúA very fair share,‚Äù said the Vicar. ‚ÄúIs it new to you?‚Äù

‚ÄúQuite,‚Äù said the Angel. ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt like it.‚Äù

‚ÄúHow curious!‚Äù said the Vicar, and bit at the end of a strip of linen to tie a knot. ‚ÄúI think this bandaging must serve for the present,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúI‚Äôve studied ambulance work before, but never the bandaging up of wing wounds. Is your pain any better?‚Äù

‚ÄúIt glows now instead of flashing,‚Äù said the Angel.

‚ÄúI am afraid you will find it glow for some time,‚Äù said the Vicar, still intent on the wound.

The Angel gave a shrug of the wing and turned round to look at the Vicar again. He had been trying to keep an eye on the Vicar over his shoulder during all their interview. He looked at him from top to toe with raised eyebrows and a growing smile on his beautiful soft-featured face. ‚ÄúIt seems so odd,‚Äù he said with a sweet little laugh, ‚Äúto be talking to a Man!‚Äù

‚ÄúDo you know,‚Äù said the Vicar, ‚Äúnow that I come to think of it, it is equally odd to me that I should be talking to an Angel. I am a somewhat matter-of-fact person. A Vicar has to be. Angels I have always regarded asÔªø‚Äîartistic conceptionsÔªø‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúExactly what we think of men.‚Äù

‚ÄúBut surely you have seen so many menÔªø‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúNever before today. In pictures and books, times enough of course. But I have seen several since the sunrise, solid real men, besides a horse or soÔªø‚Äîthose Unicorn things you know, without hornsÔªø‚Äîand quite a number of those grotesque knobby things called ‚Äòcows.‚Äô I was naturally a little frightened at so many mythical monsters, and came to hide here until it was dark. I suppose it will be dark again presently like it was at first. Phew! This Pain of yours is poor fun. I hope I shall wake up directly.‚Äù

‚ÄúI don‚Äôt understand quite,‚Äù said the Vicar, knitting his brows and tapping his forehead with his flat hand. ‚ÄúMythical monster!‚Äù The worst thing he had been called for years hitherto was a ‚Äúmedieval anachronism‚Äù (by an advocate of Disestablishment). ‚ÄúDo I understand that you consider me asÔªø‚Äîas something in a dream?‚Äù

‚ÄúOf course,‚Äù said the Angel smiling.

‚ÄúAnd this world about me, these rugged trees and spreading frondsÔªø‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúIs all so very dreamlike,‚Äù said the Angel. ‚ÄúJust exactly what one dreams ofÔªø‚Äîor artists imagine.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou have artists then among the Angels?‚Äù

‚ÄúAll kinds of artists, Angels with wonderful imaginations, who invent men and cows and eagles and a thousand impossible creatures.‚Äù

‚ÄúImpossible creatures!‚Äù said the Vicar.

‚ÄúImpossible creatures,‚Äù said the Angel. ‚ÄúMyths.‚Äù

‚ÄúBut I‚Äôm real!‚Äù said the Vicar. ‚ÄúI assure you I‚Äôm real.‚Äù

The Angel shrugged his wings and winced and smiled. ‚ÄúI can always tell when I am dreaming,‚Äù he said.

‚ÄúYouÔªø‚Äîdreaming,‚Äù said the Vicar. He looked round him.

‚ÄúYou dreaming!‚Äù he repeated. His mind worked diffusely.

He held out his hand with all his fingers moving. ‚ÄúI have it!‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúI begin to see.‚Äù A really brilliant idea was dawning upon his mind. He had not studied mathematics at Cambridge for nothing, after all. ‚ÄúTell me please. Some animals of your worldÔªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶ of the Real World, real animals you know.‚Äù

‚ÄúReal animals!‚Äù said the Angel smiling. ‚ÄúWhyÔªø‚Äîthere‚Äôs Griffins and DragonsÔªø‚Äîand JabberwocksÔªø‚Äîand CherubimÔªø‚Äîand SphinxesÔªø‚Äîand the HippogriffÔªø‚Äîand MermaidsÔªø‚Äîand SatyrsÔªø‚Äîand.Ôªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶‚Äù

‚ÄúThank you,‚Äù said the Vicar as the Angel appeared to be warming to his work; ‚Äúthank you. That is quite enough. I begin to understand.‚Äù

He paused for a moment, his face pursed up. ‚ÄúYesÔªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶ I begin to see it.‚Äù

‚ÄúSee what?‚Äù asked the Angel.

‚ÄúThe Griffins and Satyrs and so forth. It‚Äôs as clear.Ôªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶‚Äù

‚ÄúI don‚Äôt see them,‚Äù said the Angel.

‚ÄúNo, the whole point is they are not to be seen in this world. But our men with imaginations have told us all about them, you know. And even I at timesÔªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶ there are places in this village where you must simply take what they set before you, or give offenceÔªø‚ÄîI, I say, have seen in my dreams Jabberwocks, Bogle brutes, Mandrakes.Ôªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶ From our point of view, you know, they are Dream Creatures.Ôªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶‚Äù

‚ÄúDream Creatures!‚Äù said the Angel. ‚ÄúHow singular! This is a very curious dream. A kind of topsy-turvey one. You call men real and angels a myth. It almost makes one think that in some odd way there must be two worlds as it were.Ôªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶‚Äù

‚ÄúAt least two,‚Äù said the Vicar.

‚ÄúLying somewhere close together, and yet scarcely suspecting.Ôªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶‚Äù

‚ÄúAs near as page to page of a book.‚Äù

‚ÄúPenetrating each other, living each its own life. This is really a delicious dream!‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd never dreaming of each other.‚Äù

‚ÄúExcept when people go a-dreaming!‚Äù

‚ÄúYes,‚Äù said the Angel thoughtfully. ‚ÄúIt must be something of the sort. And that reminds me. Sometimes when I have been dropping asleep, or drowsing under the noontide sun, I have seen strange corrugated faces just like yours, going by me, and trees with green leaves upon them, and such queer uneven ground as this.Ôªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶ It must be so. I have fallen into another world.‚Äù

‚ÄúSometimes,‚Äù began the Vicar, ‚Äúat bedtime, when I have been just on the edge of consciousness, I have seen faces as beautiful as yours, and the strange dazzling vistas of a wonderful scene, that flowed past me, winged shapes soaring over it, and wonderfulÔªø‚Äîsometimes terribleÔªø‚Äîforms going to and fro. I have even heard sweet music too in my ears.Ôªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶ It may be that as we withdraw our attention from the world of sense, the pressing world about us, as we pass into the twilight of repose, other worlds.Ôªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶ Just as we see the stars, those other worlds in space, when the glare of day recedes.Ôªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶ And the artistic dreamers who see such things most clearly.Ôªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶‚Äù

They looked at one another.

‚ÄúAnd in some incomprehensible manner I have fallen into this world of yours out of my own!‚Äù said the Angel, ‚Äúinto the world of my dreams, grown real.‚Äù

He looked about him. ‚ÄúInto the world of my dreams.‚Äù

‚ÄúIt is confusing,‚Äù said the Vicar. ‚ÄúIt almost makes one think there may be (ahem) four dimensions after all. In which case, of course,‚Äù he went on hurriedlyÔªø‚Äîfor he loved geometrical speculations and took a certain pride in his knowledge of themÔªø‚Äî‚Äúthere may be any number of three-dimensional universes packed side by side, and all dimly dreaming of one another. There may be world upon world, universe upon universe. It‚Äôs perfectly possible. There‚Äôs nothing so incredible as the absolutely possible. But I wonder how you came to fall out of your world into mine.Ôªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶‚Äù

‚ÄúDear me!‚Äù said the Angel; ‚ÄúThere‚Äôs deer and a stag! Just as they draw them on the coats of arms. How grotesque it all seems! Can I really be awake?‚Äù

He rubbed his knuckles into his eyes.

The half-dozen of dappled deer came in Indian file obliquely through the trees and halted, watching. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs no dreamÔªø‚ÄîI am really a solid concrete Angel, in Dream Land,‚Äù said the Angel. He laughed. The Vicar stood surveying him. The Reverend gentleman was pulling his mouth askew after a habit he had, and slowly stroking his chin. He was asking himself whether he too was not in the Land of Dreams.
